content:
===BEGIN===
The changing seasons on Earth are caused by the tilt of our planet’s axis as it orbits the Sun. Imagine the Earth as a spinning top, tilted about 23.5 degrees from the vertical. This tilt means that during different times of the year, either the Northern or Southern Hemisphere leans closer to the Sun. When a hemisphere tilts toward the Sun, it experiences longer, warmer days — what we call summer. When it tilts away, the days are shorter and cooler, leading to winter. Spring and autumn occur when the tilt is such that both hemispheres receive roughly equal sunlight. This natural cycle of tilt and orbit creates predictable changes in temperature and daylight, shaping the seasons we experience. Understanding this helps businesses plan for seasonal demands, energy use, and more, making it a simple yet powerful insight into the rhythm of our planet.  
===END===
